meta.name: IELTS Band 8+ Curated
meta.note: Client-side dictionary cache optional for extra senses.

entries.def: Present, appearing, or found everywhere.
entries.example: Mobile phones have become ubiquitous in modern society.
entries.ieltsExample: The report argues that social media is now ubiquitous across all age groups.

entries.def: To make something less severe, serious, or painful.
entries.example: Governments must act now to mitigate the effects of climate change.
entries.ieltsExample: Stricter regulations could help mitigate the environmental impact of industrial waste.

entries.def: To increase rapidly in number; to spread widely.
entries.example: Social media platforms have proliferated over the past decade.
entries.ieltsExample: As online businesses proliferate, traditional retail faces increasing pressure.

entries.def: Forming a united whole; well-integrated and consistent.
entries.example: A cohesive argument is essential for a high band score in writing.
entries.ieltsExample: The government aims to build a more cohesive multicultural society.

entries.def: Causing harm or damage.
entries.example: Excessive screen time can be detrimental to children's development.
entries.ieltsExample: The study revealed that air pollution has a detrimental effect on cognitive function.

entries.def: To make a problem, bad situation, or negative feeling worse.
entries.example: The drought has exacerbated the food crisis in the region.
entries.ieltsExample: Poor urban planning often exacerbates traffic congestion in major cities.

entries.def: Mainly; for the most part.
entries.example: The population in this city is predominantly young.
entries.ieltsExample: The workforce in this sector is predominantly composed of university graduates.

entries.def: Certain to happen and impossible to avoid.
entries.example: Some argue that globalisation is inevitable in the modern world.
entries.ieltsExample: Technological unemployment may be an inevitable consequence of automation.

entries.def: A difference between two things that should be the same.
entries.example: There is a significant discrepancy between the two sets of data.
entries.ieltsExample: The discrepancy between rich and poor nations continues to widen.

entries.def: To provide evidence to support or prove the truth of something.
entries.example: You must substantiate your claims with specific examples.
entries.ieltsExample: The researcher failed to substantiate her hypothesis with sufficient data.

entries.def: Open to more than one interpretation; unclear.
entries.example: The question was ambiguous, leading to many different answers.
entries.ieltsExample: The policy statement was criticised for its ambiguous wording.

entries.def: A typical example or pattern; a worldview underlying theories of a field.
entries.example: The internet created a paradigm shift in how we communicate.
entries.ieltsExample: Electric vehicles represent a paradigm shift in the automotive industry.

entries.def: Never having happened or existed before.
entries.example: The pandemic caused unprecedented disruption to global supply chains.
entries.ieltsExample: The speed of technological change is unprecedented in human history.

entries.def: To make an action or process easier.
entries.example: Technology facilitates communication across long distances.
entries.ieltsExample: Online platforms facilitate collaboration between researchers worldwide.

entries.def: A person or thing that causes an important change or event.
entries.example: The financial crisis acted as a catalyst for economic reform.
entries.ieltsExample: Education is often the catalyst for social mobility in developing countries.

entries.def: Liable to change rapidly and unpredictably.
entries.example: Oil prices are highly volatile and hard to predict.
entries.ieltsExample: Political instability has created a volatile economic climate.

entries.def: To make something less severe.
entries.example: Aid organisations work to alleviate suffering in conflict zones.
entries.ieltsExample: Investment in public transport can alleviate traffic congestion significantly.

entries.def: A great difference or inequality between things.
entries.example: There is a growing disparity between rural and urban incomes.
entries.ieltsExample: The report highlights the health disparity between high and low-income groups.

entries.def: Based on observation or experiment rather than theory.
entries.example: We need empirical evidence before drawing any conclusions.
entries.ieltsExample: The claim lacks empirical support and remains largely theoretical.

entries.def: Dealing with things sensibly and realistically.
entries.example: A pragmatic approach to policy-making is often more effective than idealism.
entries.ieltsExample: Businesses must adopt a pragmatic strategy to survive in competitive markets.

entries.def: Capable of working successfully; feasible.
entries.example: Solar energy is now a viable alternative to fossil fuels.
entries.ieltsExample: Public transport is only viable when it is affordable and efficient.

entries.def: To become progressively worse.
entries.example: Air quality in the city has deteriorated significantly over the past decade.
entries.ieltsExample: Without investment, public infrastructure will continue to deteriorate.

entries.def: Of considerable importance, size, or worth.
entries.example: The project requires a substantial amount of funding.
entries.ieltsExample: There is substantial evidence that diet affects long-term health outcomes.

entries.def: To publicly recommend or support.
entries.example: Many scientists advocate for urgent action on climate change.
entries.ieltsExample: The report advocates a complete ban on single-use plastics.

entries.def: The ability to be maintained without depleting natural resources.
entries.example: Businesses must balance profit with long-term sustainability.
entries.ieltsExample: The concept of sustainability has moved to the centre of corporate strategy.

entries.def: To treat a person or group as insignificant or peripheral.
entries.example: Economic inequality can marginalise whole communities.
entries.ieltsExample: Social media algorithms may marginalise minority viewpoints.

entries.def: To put a decision, plan, or agreement into effect.
entries.example: The school plans to implement a new curriculum next year.
entries.ieltsExample: Many countries struggle to implement environmental legislation effectively.

entries.def: A thing that motivates or encourages someone to do something.
entries.example: Tax incentives encourage businesses to invest in renewable energy.
entries.ieltsExample: Without economic incentives, it is difficult to persuade companies to reduce emissions.

entries.def: Open, frank, and honest; easy to see through or detect.
entries.example: The company committed to being more transparent about its operations.
entries.ieltsExample: Citizens demand that their governments be more transparent in decision-making.

entries.def: The action or process of innovating; a new method or idea.
entries.example: Innovation in medicine has extended human life expectancy significantly.
entries.ieltsExample: Governments should invest in education to foster a culture of innovation.

entries.def: Able to withstand or recover quickly from difficult conditions.
entries.example: A resilient economy can absorb shocks without long-term damage.
entries.ieltsExample: Cities must become more resilient to the effects of climate change.

entries.def: General agreement among a group of people.
entries.example: There is a scientific consensus that human activity is causing climate change.
entries.ieltsExample: Policymakers must build consensus before implementing major reforms.

entries.def: To examine or inspect closely and thoroughly.
entries.example: Investors scrutinise company accounts before making decisions.
entries.ieltsExample: Government spending should be regularly scrutinised by independent bodies.

entries.def: Evoking interest or admiration in a powerfully irresistible way.
entries.example: She made a compelling argument for reform.
entries.ieltsExample: The report presents compelling evidence that diet is linked to mental wellbeing.

entries.def: To weaken or damage gradually.
entries.example: Corruption undermines public trust in government institutions.
entries.ieltsExample: Fake news can undermine the credibility of legitimate scientific research.

entries.def: To destroy or remove completely.
entries.example: The WHO aims to eradicate polio from every country in the world.
entries.ieltsExample: Without international cooperation, it is impossible to eradicate climate change.

entries.def: Clear and definite; able to be touched or felt.
entries.example: The new policy has produced tangible improvements in air quality.
entries.ieltsExample: Governments must deliver tangible results, not just empty promises.

entries.def: To make something continue indefinitely.
entries.example: Biased media can perpetuate harmful stereotypes.
entries.ieltsExample: A lack of quality education perpetuates cycles of poverty across generations.

entries.def: Language designed to persuade; often insincere or empty speech.
entries.example: The politician's speech was full of rhetoric but short on detail.
entries.ieltsExample: Leaders must move beyond rhetoric and take concrete action on climate change.

entries.def: Possible to do easily or conveniently.
entries.example: Renewable energy is now a technically and economically feasible alternative.
entries.ieltsExample: A zero-carbon economy is feasible within the next thirty years if governments act decisively.

entries.def: In addition; besides — used to introduce a further point.
entries.example: The plan is costly. Furthermore, it has little public support.
entries.ieltsExample: The policy reduces emissions. Furthermore, it creates thousands of jobs.

entries.def: In spite of that; notwithstanding; all the same.
entries.example: The task was difficult. Nevertheless, they completed it on time.
entries.ieltsExample: The cost is high. Nevertheless, the long-term benefits justify the investment.

entries.def: As a result; therefore.
entries.example: The harvest failed; consequently, food prices rose sharply.
entries.ieltsExample: Investment fell. Consequently, unemployment rose to a record high.

entries.def: When compared with something else, showing a difference.
entries.example: In contrast to urban areas, rural regions saw little growth.
entries.ieltsExample: In contrast to previous decades, modern workers value flexibility over job security.

entries.def: In spite of; despite.
entries.example: Notwithstanding the difficulties, the project was a success.
entries.ieltsExample: Notwithstanding the economic downturn, some sectors continued to grow.

entries.def: Used to introduce a personal opinion.
collocations: in my view, the most important
in my view, this is because
entries.example: In my view, education should be free for all.
entries.ieltsExample: In my view, governments bear the greatest responsibility for tackling climate change.

entries.def: Partly; in some degree, but not completely.
entries.example: To a certain extent, the results were predictable.
entries.ieltsExample: To a certain extent, social media has improved global communication.

entries.def: It is obvious; it does not need to be stated.
entries.example: It goes without saying that honesty is the best policy.
entries.ieltsExample: It goes without saying that access to clean water is a fundamental human right.

entries.def: Used to present two contrasting aspects of an argument.
collocations: on the one hand... on the other
entries.example: On the one hand, technology creates jobs; on the other, it destroys them.
entries.ieltsExample: On the one hand, nuclear energy is clean; on the other, waste disposal is problematic.

entries.def: In my opinion; from my perspective.
collocations: as far as I'm concerned, the key issue
entries.example: As far as I'm concerned, kindness is the most important quality a person can have.
entries.ieltsExample: As far as I'm concerned, the benefits of technology far outweigh the drawbacks.

entries.def: To be the reason for; to constitute a proportion of something.
entries.example: Technology accounts for a large proportion of GDP in developed countries.
entries.ieltsExample: Carbon dioxide accounts for the majority of greenhouse gas emissions.

entries.def: To cause or produce something.
entries.example: Rapid urbanisation gives rise to increased demand for housing.
entries.ieltsExample: The growth of social media has given rise to new forms of communication and misinformation.

entries.def: To use as a source; to rely on.
entries.example: Good writers draw on a wide range of vocabulary.
entries.ieltsExample: The report draws on data from over 50 countries to support its conclusions.

entries.def: To use as a foundation for further development.
entries.example: The new strategy builds on the success of last year's initiative.
entries.ieltsExample: Developing countries must build on their existing economic strengths.

entries.def: To perform or conduct a task or action.
entries.example: The scientists carried out extensive research before publishing their findings.
entries.ieltsExample: Government agencies must carry out regular inspections of food production facilities.

entries.def: To confirm or support with evidence.
entries.example: Witnesses corroborated his account of the events.
entries.ieltsExample: Independent studies corroborate the link between sleep deprivation and poor academic performance.

entries.def: To describe or portray precisely.
entries.example: The contract delineates each party's responsibilities.
entries.ieltsExample: The introduction should delineate the main issues the essay will address.

entries.def: To make something clear; to explain.
entries.example: The professor elucidated the theory with simple examples.
entries.ieltsExample: The conclusion elucidates why the proposed policy is preferable.

entries.def: Most noticeable or important.
entries.example: Cost remains the most salient concern for consumers.
entries.ieltsExample: The most salient difference between the two approaches is long-term sustainability.

entries.def: A complex consequence of an action.
entries.example: The decision had serious ramifications for the whole industry.
entries.ieltsExample: Governments must consider the social ramifications of automation.

entries.def: Spreading widely throughout an area or group.
entries.example: Social media has a pervasive influence on young people.
entries.ieltsExample: Plastic pollution is now pervasive in marine ecosystems worldwide.

entries.def: Not securely held or in position; dangerously uncertain.
entries.example: Many workers face precarious employment conditions.
entries.ieltsExample: Climate change puts coastal communities in a precarious position.

entries.def: Widespread in a particular area or at a particular time.
entries.example: Obesity is increasingly prevalent among adolescents.
entries.ieltsExample: Remote working has become prevalent since the pandemic.

entries.def: Existing as a permanent or essential attribute.
entries.example: There are inherent risks in any surgical procedure.
entries.ieltsExample: Tourism carries inherent environmental costs that must be managed.

entries.def: Belonging naturally; essential.
entries.example: Some argue that art has intrinsic value beyond profit.
entries.ieltsExample: Learning a language offers intrinsic rewards beyond career prospects.

entries.def: Making a situation or outcome likely or possible.
entries.example: Quiet spaces are conducive to concentration.
entries.ieltsExample: Flexible hours are conducive to better work–life balance.

entries.def: Causing or likely to cause disagreement.
entries.example: Immigration remains a contentious political topic.
entries.ieltsExample: Nuclear energy is a contentious subject in environmental debates.

entries.def: Inspiring fear or respect through being impressively capable.
entries.example: She is a formidable competitor in international markets.
entries.ieltsExample: Reducing carbon emissions is a formidable task for any government.

entries.def: About to happen.
entries.example: They prepared for the imminent storm.
entries.ieltsExample: Experts warn of imminent water shortages in several regions.

entries.def: Of vital importance; crucial.
entries.example: It is imperative that we reduce waste.
entries.ieltsExample: Investing in renewable energy is an economic imperative.

entries.def: Seeming reasonable or probable.
entries.example: Her explanation sounded plausible at first.
entries.ieltsExample: The report offers a plausible account of rising inequality.

entries.def: Strong and healthy; vigorous.
entries.example: The company reported robust quarterly earnings.
entries.ieltsExample: A robust public health system can withstand future crises.

entries.def: Showing no activity; dull and sluggish.
entries.example: Wages have remained stagnant for years.
entries.ieltsExample: Without reform, productivity growth may remain stagnant.

entries.def: Likely or vulnerable to be influenced or harmed.
entries.example: Children are more susceptible to infections.
entries.ieltsExample: Coastal areas are especially susceptible to flooding.

entries.def: Persistent; not readily letting go.
entries.example: She is tenacious in pursuing her goals.
entries.ieltsExample: Tenacious advocacy helped secure stronger environmental laws.

entries.def: Able to adapt to many functions or activities.
entries.example: The software is versatile enough for many industries.
entries.ieltsExample: A versatile education prepares graduates for changing labour markets.

entries.def: To support or strengthen.
entries.example: The news bolstered investor confidence.
entries.ieltsExample: Subsidies can bolster innovation in clean technology.

entries.def: To reach a climax or point of highest development.
entries.example: Years of research culminated in a breakthrough.
entries.ieltsExample: The project culminated in a landmark international agreement.

entries.def: To involve as a necessary part or consequence.
entries.example: The role entails frequent travel abroad.
entries.ieltsExample: Banning cars from city centres would entail major infrastructure changes.

entries.def: To establish firmly so that change is difficult.
entries.example: Old habits are deeply entrenched in the organisation.
entries.ieltsExample: Poor funding can entrench educational inequality across regions.

entries.def: To be a typical example of.
entries.example: The case exemplifies broader problems in the sector.
entries.ieltsExample: Nordic welfare models exemplify successful social investment.

entries.def: To prove a statement or theory wrong.
entries.example: New data refuted the earlier hypothesis.
entries.ieltsExample: Critics attempted to refute the study's methodology.

entries.def: To say something again for emphasis.
entries.example: The minister reiterated the government's commitment.
entries.ieltsExample: The essay should reiterate the thesis without introducing new ideas.

entries.def: To demand or specify as part of an agreement.
entries.example: The contract stipulates a thirty-day notice period.
entries.ieltsExample: Regulations stipulate minimum safety standards for all vehicles.

entries.def: To increase suddenly and powerfully.
entries.example: Demand for electric vehicles has surged.
entries.ieltsExample: Energy prices surged following the supply disruption.

entries.def: To attribute something to a stated cause.
entries.example: He ascribed his success to hard work.
entries.ieltsExample: Many failures are ascribed to poor planning rather than bad luck.

entries.def: An opinion formed without sufficient evidence.
entries.example: Their claims were based on conjecture, not data.
entries.ieltsExample: Until further research is done, any figure remains conjecture.

entries.def: Written or spoken communication on a subject.
entries.example: Climate change dominates public discourse today.
entries.ieltsExample: Media discourse shapes how societies view immigration.

entries.def: A proposed explanation made on limited evidence.
entries.example: Scientists tested the hypothesis in controlled trials.
entries.ieltsExample: The hypothesis that homework improves outcomes remains contested.

entries.def: A difficult or unpleasant situation.
entries.example: The company found itself in a financial predicament.
entries.ieltsExample: Developing nations face a predicament between growth and emissions.

entries.def: Nearness in space, time, or relationship.
entries.example: Housing costs rise in proximity to the city centre.
entries.ieltsExample: Proximity to universities attracts technology firms.

entries.def: Critical observation or examination.
entries.example: The policy came under intense public scrutiny.
entries.ieltsExample: Corporate tax arrangements deserve greater parliamentary scrutiny.

entries.def: An amount left over when requirements have been met.
entries.example: The nation reported a trade surplus last year.
entries.ieltsExample: Agricultural surpluses can depress prices for farmers.

entries.def: The path followed by something; course of development.
entries.example: The startup is on an upward trajectory.
entries.ieltsExample: Current policies put emissions on a dangerous trajectory.

entries.def: A thing that provides resistance or delay.
entries.example: Lack of funding was a major hindrance to progress.
entries.ieltsExample: Bureaucracy can be a hindrance to small business growth.

entries.def: Harm or damage.
entries.example: Long hours work to the detriment of family life.
entries.ieltsExample: Over-tourism can be to the detriment of local culture.

entries.def: To display or show clearly.
entries.example: Stress can manifest as physical illness.
entries.ieltsExample: Inequality manifests in unequal access to healthcare.

entries.def: To cause to become; to provide or give.
entries.example: Automation may render some jobs obsolete.
entries.ieltsExample: Poor maintenance can render public transport unreliable.

entries.def: To justify or necessitate.
entries.example: The results warrant further research.
entries.ieltsExample: Rising crime rates warrant a review of policing strategy.

entries.def: Lasting for a very short time.
entries.example: Fame on social media can be ephemeral.
entries.ieltsExample: Many digital trends prove ephemeral within months.

entries.def: Not able to be seen through; unclear.
entries.example: The decision-making process remained opaque.
entries.ieltsExample: Opaque pricing makes it hard for consumers to compare services.

entries.def: No longer produced or used; out of date.
entries.example: Floppy disks are now obsolete technology.
entries.ieltsExample: Traditional retail models risk becoming obsolete.

entries.def: Having a limited outlook; narrow-minded.
entries.example: We must avoid parochial thinking in global policy.
entries.ieltsExample: Parochial education policies ignore international best practice.

entries.def: Not easily convinced; having doubts.
entries.example: Investors remain skeptical about the merger.
entries.ieltsExample: Many citizens are skeptical of politicians' climate promises.

entries.def: Occurring at irregular intervals.
entries.example: The region suffers intermittent power cuts.
entries.ieltsExample: Intermittent lockdowns disrupted education worldwide.

entries.def: Necessary to make a whole complete; essential.
entries.example: Trust is integral to any partnership.
entries.ieltsExample: Technology is now integral to modern classroom teaching.

entries.def: Producing a great deal of profit.
entries.example: Tech offers lucrative career paths for graduates.
entries.ieltsExample: Tourism can be lucrative but environmentally costly.

entries.def: Famous for something bad.
entries.example: The road is notorious for accidents.
entries.ieltsExample: The city is notorious for traffic congestion.

entries.def: A means of counteracting or eliminating something undesirable.
entries.example: Education is often seen as a remedy for poverty.
entries.ieltsExample: There is no simple remedy for urban overcrowding.

entries.def: To produce or generate in large numbers.
entries.example: The scandal spawned numerous investigations.
entries.ieltsExample: The internet has spawned new forms of entrepreneurship.

entries.def: To forcibly put an end to; to prevent development.
entries.example: The regime tried to suppress free speech.
entries.ieltsExample: High taxes may suppress small business growth.

entries.def: Not varying; the same in all cases.
entries.example: Schools adopted uniform dress codes.
entries.ieltsExample: A uniform curriculum may not suit diverse learners.

entries.def: To clear of blame or suspicion; to justify.
entries.example: The results vindicated years of research.
entries.ieltsExample: Later evidence vindicated early warnings about the crisis.

entries.def: A particular sector of a population.
entries.example: Advertisers target specific demographics.
entries.ieltsExample: An ageing demographic poses challenges for healthcare systems.

entries.def: Not resulting from or achieved through deliberate planning.
entries.example: The breach was caused by inadvertent human error.
entries.ieltsExample: Inadvertent policy gaps can widen inequality.

entries.def: Best or most favourable.
entries.example: Plants grow best under optimal conditions.
entries.ieltsExample: Governments seek the optimal balance between growth and protection.

entries.def: A tendency to choose or do something regularly.
entries.example: He has a proclivity for risk-taking.
entries.ieltsExample: Humans have a proclivity to favour short-term rewards.

entries.def: Lower in rank or position.
entries.example: Women were often given subordinate roles historically.
entries.ieltsExample: Economic concerns should not be subordinate to environmental protection—or vice versa without debate.

entries.def: Very weak or slight.
entries.example: The link between the events is tenuous.
entries.ieltsExample: The argument rests on tenuous statistical evidence.

entries.def: Not confined or restricted.
entries.example: They demanded unfettered access to information.
entries.ieltsExample: Unfettered development can damage fragile ecosystems.

entries.def: To waver between different opinions or actions.
entries.example: The committee vacillated for months before deciding.
entries.ieltsExample: Governments that vacillate on climate policy lose public trust.

entries.def: The act of making something worse.
entries.example: Stress led to an exacerbation of her condition.
entries.ieltsExample: War caused an exacerbation of the refugee crisis.

entries.def: The fact of placing things close together for contrast.
entries.example: The film uses juxtaposition of rich and poor districts.
entries.ieltsExample: The juxtaposition of wealth and poverty is stark in many capitals.

entries.def: Establishing a standard; prescriptive.
entries.example: The study questions normative views of gender roles.
entries.ieltsExample: Normative claims about how people ought to live are debated in ethics.

entries.def: Relevant or applicable to a particular matter.
entries.example: Please keep your comments pertinent to the topic.
entries.ieltsExample: Only pertinent examples should appear in the body paragraphs.

entries.def: Having a great deal of money; wealthy.
entries.example: They live in an affluent suburb.
entries.ieltsExample: Affluent consumers drive demand for luxury goods.

entries.def: Growing or developing rapidly.
entries.example: The city has a burgeoning tech scene.
entries.ieltsExample: Burgeoning urban populations strain public services.

entries.def: Standing out so as to be clearly visible.
entries.example: His wealth was displayed in conspicuous consumption.
entries.ieltsExample: The conspicuous gap between rich and poor fuels resentment.

entries.def: Seeming difficult to deal with; intimidating.
entries.example: Starting a business can be daunting.
entries.ieltsExample: Learning academic writing is daunting for many candidates.

entries.def: Outstandingly bad; shocking.
entries.example: The report revealed egregious safety violations.
entries.ieltsExample: Egregious inequality undermines social cohesion.

entries.def: Sparing or economical with money or resources.
entries.example: Her frugal habits helped her save quickly.
entries.ieltsExample: Frugal energy use benefits both households and the planet.

entries.def: Fond of company; sociable.
entries.example: She has a gregarious personality.
entries.ieltsExample: Gregarious students often thrive in discussion-based classes.

entries.def: Lacking organisation or planning.
entries.example: The files were stored in a haphazard way.
entries.ieltsExample: Haphazard urban planning creates long-term infrastructure problems.

entries.def: Treating all rivals or disputants equally.
entries.example: Judges must remain impartial.
entries.ieltsExample: Examiners are trained to give impartial feedback.

entries.def: Showing great attention to detail.
entries.example: She is meticulous about proofreading.
entries.ieltsExample: Meticulous Task 1 descriptions can lift band scores.

entries.def: A subtle difference in meaning or expression.
entries.example: The translation missed important nuance.
entries.ieltsExample: Strong essays capture nuance rather than oversimplifying issues.

entries.def: Involving a great deal of effort or difficulty.
entries.example: The tax forms were onerous to complete.
entries.ieltsExample: Onerous visa requirements deter skilled migrants.

entries.def: Producing much; abundantly fruitful.
entries.example: She is a prolific author of research papers.
entries.ieltsExample: China has become a prolific producer of renewable technology.

entries.def: Representing the most perfect example.
entries.example: Fish and chips is quintessential British cuisine.
entries.ieltsExample: Paris is often seen as the quintessential romantic city.

entries.def: No longer needed or useful.
entries.example: Several staff were made redundant last year.
entries.ieltsExample: Automation may render some skills redundant.

entries.def: Severe or bare in appearance; blunt.
entries.example: There is a stark contrast between rich and poor areas.
entries.ieltsExample: The data presents a stark picture of climate impact.

entries.def: Necessary as a duty or responsibility.
entries.example: It is incumbent on leaders to act responsibly.
entries.ieltsExample: It is incumbent on policymakers to protect vulnerable groups.

entries.def: The fact of appearing everywhere.
entries.example: We discuss the ubiquity of digital advertising.
entries.ieltsExample: The ubiquity of English gives native speakers an advantage abroad.

entries.def: Accorded great respect due to age or wisdom.
entries.example: The university is a venerable institution.
entries.ieltsExample: Venerable customs may still hinder gender equality.

entries.def: On a large scale; complete.
entries.example: The company announced wholesale restructuring.
entries.ieltsExample: Wholesale adoption of electric buses could cut urban emissions.

entries.def: Severe or strict in manner; having no comforts.
entries.example: They lived under austere economic conditions.
entries.ieltsExample: Austerity programmes impose austere spending limits on welfare.

entries.def: Abundant in supply or quantity.
entries.example: She took copious notes during the lecture.
entries.ieltsExample: Copious evidence supports the benefits of early childhood education.

entries.def: Hesitating or doubting; not to be relied upon.
entries.example: I am dubious about their promises.
entries.ieltsExample: Many remain dubious of quick-fix solutions to climate change.

entries.def: Lasting for a very short time.
entries.example: Happiness can be a fleeting feeling.
entries.ieltsExample: Social media offers only fleeting satisfaction for some users.

entries.def: Dignity, seriousness, or solemnity of manner.
entries.example: The leader spoke with real gravitas.
entries.ieltsExample: Academic writing benefits from gravitas without being pompous.

entries.def: As a further matter; besides.
entries.example: The plan is expensive. Moreover, it may fail.
entries.ieltsExample: Renewable energy reduces emissions. Moreover, it creates jobs.

entries.def: As a consequence; for this reason.
entries.example: The data was incomplete; hence the delay.
entries.ieltsExample: Investment fell; hence unemployment rose sharply.

entries.def: As a result or consequence; therefore.
entries.example: He studied hard and thus passed easily.
entries.ieltsExample: Costs have risen; thus consumers seek cheaper alternatives.

entries.def: In a way that is appropriate to the circumstances.
entries.example: Rules changed and staff acted accordingly.
entries.ieltsExample: Governments must legislate accordingly to meet climate targets.

entries.def: In an opposite or reversed way.
entries.example: Urban areas grew; conversely, rural populations shrank.
entries.ieltsExample: Wealth rose in cities; conversely, rural incomes stagnated.

entries.def: In the same way; also.
entries.example: She excelled in maths; likewise in physics.
entries.ieltsExample: Japan invested in rail; likewise, France expanded its network.

entries.def: After a particular thing has happened.
entries.example: He resigned and subsequently moved abroad.
entries.ieltsExample: The law passed and subsequently reduced smoking rates.

entries.def: Mainly; above all.
entries.example: The delay was chiefly caused by weather.
entries.ieltsExample: The decline was chiefly due to falling investment.

entries.def: In every case or on every occasion.
entries.example: The test invariably proves difficult.
entries.ieltsExample: Poor planning invariably leads to weaker essays.

entries.def: Used to introduce a concession.
entries.example: Admittedly, the cost is high.
entries.ieltsExample: Admittedly, technology has drawbacks; nevertheless, benefits outweigh them.

entries.def: Used to concede a point before making another.
entries.example: Granted, the policy is costly, but it is necessary.
entries.ieltsExample: Granted, tourism creates jobs; however, it can harm the environment.

entries.def: Used to highlight a difference.
entries.example: Cities grew rapidly. By contrast, villages declined.
entries.ieltsExample: By contrast, renewable investment doubled in the same period.

entries.def: Taking everything into consideration.
entries.example: On balance, the project was worthwhile.
entries.ieltsExample: On balance, remote work benefits outweigh the drawbacks.

entries.def: For that purpose.
entries.example: To that end, they hired more teachers.
entries.ieltsExample: To that end, governments fund teacher training programmes.

entries.def: Because of new information or circumstances.
entries.example: In light of new evidence, they reopened the case.
entries.ieltsExample: In light of climate data, stricter regulations are justified.

entries.def: Regarding; concerning.
entries.example: With respect to safety, changes are needed.
entries.ieltsExample: With respect to education, funding remains inadequate.

entries.def: To the extent that.
entries.example: Help will be given insofar as funds allow.
entries.ieltsExample: Technology helps insofar as users develop digital literacy.

entries.def: Especially; particularly important.
entries.example: The plan matters, not least for the poor.
entries.ieltsExample: Education reform matters, not least for rural communities.

entries.def: Nevertheless; however (after a concession).
entries.example: The film was long. That said, it was engaging.
entries.ieltsExample: Tourism boosts income. That said, it can damage heritage sites.

entries.def: Taking all factors into account.
entries.example: All things considered, we made the right choice.
entries.ieltsExample: All things considered, nuclear power remains controversial.

entries.def: Used to present a debatable claim.
entries.example: It is arguable that the policy failed.
entries.ieltsExample: It is arguable that free university education benefits society overall.

entries.def: Used to express a tentative personal view.
collocations: I am inclined to believe that
entries.example: I am inclined to believe he is right.
entries.ieltsExample: I am inclined to believe that prevention is cheaper than cure.

entries.def: Used to say something must be mentioned.
collocations: it would be remiss to ignore
entries.example: It would be remiss to ignore safety concerns.
entries.ieltsExample: It would be remiss to overlook the role of poverty in crime.

entries.def: Used to acknowledge a valid point.
entries.example: There is merit in both proposals.
entries.ieltsExample: There is merit in restricting cars in city centres.

entries.def: Used to state a weighed final position.
entries.example: On balance, I contend that reform is necessary.
entries.ieltsExample: On balance, I contend that governments should regulate social media.

entries.def: Used to caution against oversimplification.
entries.example: It is tempting to assume success was inevitable.
entries.ieltsExample: It is tempting to assume technology alone solves education gaps.

entries.def: Used for widely accepted claims.
entries.example: Few would dispute that health matters.
entries.ieltsExample: Few would dispute that climate change poses serious risks.

entries.def: Used to present a moderate claim.
entries.example: It is not unreasonable to ask for proof.
entries.ieltsExample: It is not unreasonable to expect cleaner air in cities.

entries.def: Used to offer a careful opinion.
entries.example: I would venture that costs will rise.
entries.ieltsExample: I would venture that cultural exchange fosters mutual understanding.

entries.def: The most important point.
collocations: the crux of the matter is
entries.example: The crux of the matter is funding.
entries.ieltsExample: The crux of the matter is whether growth can be sustainable.

entries.def: In my opinion.
entries.example: To my mind, the film was overrated.
entries.ieltsExample: To my mind, public transport deserves greater investment.

entries.def: Used to state a formal personal opinion.
entries.example: I take the view that change is overdue.
entries.ieltsExample: I take the view that arts education should not be cut.

entries.def: Used when no consensus exists.
entries.example: Whether it will work is open to debate.
entries.ieltsExample: Whether homework improves learning remains open to debate.

entries.def: Used to express conviction after consideration.
entries.example: I am persuaded that the evidence is sound.
entries.ieltsExample: I am persuaded that early intervention reduces crime long-term.

entries.def: Generally; considering everything.
entries.example: On the whole, the trip was enjoyable.
entries.ieltsExample: On the whole, globalisation has lifted living standards.

entries.word: Off the top of my head
entries.def: Without careful thought or preparation.
collocations: off the top of my head, I'd say
off the top of my head
entries.example: Off the top of my head, I'd say about fifty.
entries.ieltsExample: Off the top of my head, I'd guess tourism is the main industry there.

entries.def: Sounds familiar; I think I remember.
entries.example: The name rings a bell but I can't place him.
entries.ieltsExample: Your description of the festival rings a bell — I read about it.

entries.def: Undecided between two options.
entries.example: I'm on the fence about which job to take.
entries.ieltsExample: I'm on the fence about whether cities should ban cars entirely.

entries.def: Used when something occurs to you.
collocations: now that I come to think
entries.example: Come to think of it, I did meet her once.
entries.ieltsExample: Come to think of it, my hometown has changed dramatically.

entries.def: To forget something temporarily.
entries.example: Sorry, your number slips my mind.
entries.ieltsExample: Important details sometimes slip my mind under pressure.

entries.def: In general terms; without detail.
entries.example: Broadly speaking, the plan makes sense.
entries.ieltsExample: Broadly speaking, young people are more environmentally aware.

entries.def: As far as I can remember.
entries.example: If memory serves, we met in 2019.
entries.ieltsExample: If memory serves, the course lasted six months.

entries.word: I wouldn't go so far as to say
entries.phonetic: /aɪ ˈwʊd.ənt ɡəʊ səʊ fɑː æz tuː seɪ/
entries.def: Used to moderate a strong claim.
collocations: wouldn't go so far as to
go so far as to say
entries.example: I wouldn't go so far as to call it a disaster.
entries.ieltsExample: I wouldn't go so far as to say technology ruins relationships.

entries.def: A matter open to debate or no longer relevant.
entries.example: Whether he intended it is a moot point.
entries.ieltsExample: Whether the old stadium should reopen is a moot point now.

entries.def: Used to give a tentative answer.
entries.example: I'm inclined to say she'll agree.
entries.ieltsExample: I'm inclined to say rural life is quieter but less convenient.

entries.def: To be reducible to the essential element.
entries.example: The issue boils down to trust.
entries.ieltsExample: Most environmental debates boil down to economic priorities.

entries.def: To improve knowledge by revising.
entries.example: I need to brush up on my Spanish.
entries.ieltsExample: Candidates should brush up on Task 1 chart vocabulary.

entries.def: To abolish or remove.
entries.example: They want to do away with the old law.
entries.ieltsExample: Some argue we should do away with homework entirely.

entries.def: To fail to reach a required standard.
entries.example: Sales fell short of expectations.
entries.ieltsExample: Current policies fall short of Paris Agreement targets.

entries.def: To focus attention on.
entries.example: Investigators zeroed in on the suspect.
entries.ieltsExample: Strong essays zero in on the most relevant causes.
